sources.note: Stems/options scraped verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e Review Questions. OpenStax does NOT publish the Biology 2e review-question answer key publicly, so correctAnswer is verified-from-source-text (bedrock factual/computational genetics & evolution items cross-checked against the Biology 2e chapter text). License recorded as the current official CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (originally released CC BY 4.0). OpenStax posts a 'no LLM training' notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing per PRD.

meta.notes: File 07: OpenStax Biology 2e Ch. 12 (Mendel's Experiments and Heredity) and Ch. 19 (The Evolution of Populations). One Hardy-Weinberg item (Ch.19 Q6) was skipped because two options are simultaneously out of equilibrium (ambiguous). Options 1-4 map to A-D. answerProvenance is verified-from-source-text for all items (no public OpenStax key).

questions.stem: Mendel performed hybridizations by transferring pollen from the _______ of the male plant to the female ova.
questions.explanation: Pollen is produced in the anther (the male part). Mendel transferred pollen from the anther of one plant to the carpel of another to make controlled crosses.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q5). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Pollen carries the male gametes of a flowering plant. Which general part of the flower produces pollen?
hints.rationale: Pollen is produced by the flower's male reproductive organ.

hints.prompt: Among the answer choices, 'pistil' and 'stigma' are parts of which structure?
hints.rationale: The pistil and its stigma are female structures that receive pollen rather than produce it.

hints.prompt: The blank must name the male SOURCE of the pollen. Which two options can be ruled out because they are female structures that receive pollen?
hints.rationale: Pistil and stigma are female parts that receive pollen, so neither can be the male source.

questions.stem: Which is one of the seven characteristics that Mendel observed in pea plants?
questions.explanation: Seed texture (round vs. wrinkled) is one of Mendel's seven characters. Flower size, leaf shape, and stem color were not among them.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q6). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Mendel's seven characters were each observed as two clearly contrasting, discrete forms (an either/or difference). Which of the following is an example of that kind of discrete either/or contrast?

choices.label: B
choices.text: slightly taller vs. slightly shorter than average

choices.label: D
choices.text: a smooth gradient of stem shades

hints.rationale: Mendel used traits with two distinct forms, such as purple versus white flowers, not continuously varying features.

hints.prompt: Mendel deliberately avoided traits that vary continuously (like size) and traits he did not track. Why would 'flower size' be a poor fit for his method?

choices.label: A
choices.text: because size varies continuously rather than as two discrete forms

choices.label: B
choices.text: because flowers have no heritable traits

choices.label: C
choices.text: because size is determined only by environment, never genes

hints.rationale: Mendel needed discrete either/or traits, and size varies continuously rather than in two clear forms.

hints.prompt: Mendel DID study the pea flower (its color and position), which makes one distractor especially tempting. Which option is the trap that names the right organ (the flower) but the wrong feature (a continuous size)?
hints.rationale: Mendel studied flower color and position but not flower size, making 'flower size' a tempting trap.

questions.stem: Imagine you are performing a cross involving seed color in garden pea plants. What F1 offspring would you expect if you cross true-breeding parents with green seeds and yellow seeds? Yellow seed color is dominant over green.
questions.explanation: Crossing true-breeding YY (yellow) x yy (green) yields all Yy offspring. Because yellow is dominant, 100% of the F1 are yellow.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q7). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified by Mendelian reasoning.

hints.prompt: When you cross two true-breeding (homozygous) parents that differ in one trait, what are ALL of the F1 offspring genotypically?
hints.rationale: A homozygous-dominant x homozygous-recessive cross gives uniformly heterozygous F1.

hints.prompt: Because every F1 plant here has the identical genotype (Yy), what can you conclude about the F1 phenotypes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: they will be uniform (all the same phenotype)

choices.label: D
choices.text: they will each be individually a blend of two colors

hints.rationale: Identical genotypes yield a uniform phenotype across the F1, not a ratio.

hints.prompt: Because these alleles show SIMPLE (complete) dominance rather than incomplete dominance, which answer choice is a trap describing a blended intermediate color that would only occur under incomplete dominance?
hints.rationale: A yellow-green blend would arise only under incomplete dominance, so it is the trap here.

questions.stem: Consider a cross to investigate the pea pod texture trait, involving constricted or inflated pods. Mendel found that the traits behave according to a dominant/recessive pattern in which inflated pods were dominant. If you performed this cross and obtained 650 inflated-pod plants in the F2 generation, approximately how many constricted-pod plants would you expect to have?
questions.explanation: An F2 monohybrid cross gives a 3:1 ratio (inflated:constricted). If inflated = 650 (three parts), one part = 650/3 ≈ 217 constricted plants.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q8). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified by calculation (650/3 ≈ 217).

hints.prompt: In a monohybrid F2 generation for a simple dominant/recessive trait, what is the expected phenotypic ratio of dominant to recessive?
hints.rationale: A monohybrid F2 shows a 3:1 dominant-to-recessive phenotypic ratio.

hints.prompt: In a 3:1 ratio, the 650 inflated (dominant) plants correspond to what fraction of the total F2 plants?
hints.rationale: Dominant phenotypes make up three of the four parts, i.e., three-quarters of the F2.

hints.prompt: A common mistake is to treat 650 as the TOTAL number of F2 plants rather than as the dominant (3-part) class. Which answer choice reflects that error (approximately 650 divided by 4)?
hints.rationale: Dividing 650 by 4 (about 165) wrongly treats 650 as the total, making it the trap.

questions.stem: A scientist pollinates a true-breeding pea plant with violet, terminal flowers with pollen from a true-breeding pea plant with white, axial flowers. Which of the following observations would most accurately describe the F2 generation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 75% white flowers in a terminal position

choices.label: D
choices.text: 75% violet flowers in an axial position

questions.explanation: Violet is dominant to white and axial is dominant to terminal. The F1 are all violet and axial, and each trait independently gives a 3:1 F2 ratio, so about 75% are violet and about 75% are axial.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q9). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified by Mendelian reasoning (violet and axial are the dominant pea traits).

hints.prompt: In a dihybrid cross, each trait assorts independently. For a single dominant/recessive trait, about what fraction of the F2 shows the dominant form?
hints.rationale: Each independently assorting trait yields roughly 75% dominant in the F2 (a 3:1 ratio).

hints.prompt: The violet parent had terminal flowers and the white parent had axial flowers. In the F2, does the roughly 75% axial result depend on axial having come from the white parent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because traits assort independently, the parental pairing does not matter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, axial can only appear together with white

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, traits stay linked to their original parent

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because axial is actually the recessive form

hints.rationale: Independent assortment means a trait's dominant form predominates regardless of which parent contributed it.

hints.prompt: One distractor claims '75% violet flowers in an axial position,' combining two traits into a single 75%. Why is stating a combined 75% incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: because the probability of BOTH dominant traits together is 9/16 (about 56%), not 75%

choices.label: B
choices.text: because violet is the recessive form

choices.label: C
choices.text: because axial never occurs together with violet

hints.rationale: Two independent 3/4 events give a joint probability of 9/16 (about 56%), so a combined 75% is wrong.

questions.stem: The observable traits expressed by an organism are described as its ________.
questions.explanation: The phenotype is the set of observable characteristics; the genotype is the underlying genetic makeup.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q10). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Geneticists distinguish an organism's observable characteristics from its underlying genetic makeup. The genetic makeup itself is called the:
hints.rationale: The genotype is the underlying genetic makeup, as distinct from what is observed.

hints.prompt: Two of the main answer choices, 'alleles' and 'zygote,' refer to something other than a set of observable traits. What does 'zygote' actually denote?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a fertilized egg (the first cell of a new organism)

choices.label: D
choices.text: the complete set of observed traits

hints.rationale: A zygote is a fertilized egg, not a description of observable traits.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because one names a single gene VERSION and the other names an early CELL/stage, neither of which is a description of traits?
hints.rationale: 'Alleles' are gene versions and a 'zygote' is a fertilized-egg cell, so neither describes an organism's traits.

questions.stem: A recessive trait will be observed in individuals that are ________ for that trait.
questions.explanation: A recessive phenotype appears only when the individual is homozygous recessive; a heterozygote shows the dominant phenotype.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q11). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: If an individual has one dominant and one recessive allele for a trait, which phenotype is expressed?
hints.rationale: With one dominant allele present, the dominant phenotype is expressed and the recessive is masked.

hints.prompt: One main answer choice, 'diploid,' describes having two sets of chromosomes in general. Is 'diploid' specific enough to explain WHY a recessive trait shows up?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, nearly all body cells are diploid, so it does not specify the allele combination

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, diploid means having two recessive alleles

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, only diploid cells can show recessive traits

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, diploid means having just one allele

hints.rationale: Being diploid only means two chromosome sets; it does not specify the two-recessive-allele condition needed.

hints.prompt: A heterozygote shows the DOMINANT phenotype, so a recessive trait cannot appear in heterozygotes. Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they include heterozygotes?
hints.rationale: Both 'heterozygous' and 'homozygous or heterozygous' include heterozygotes, which show the dominant trait, so both are ruled out.

questions.stem: If black and white true-breeding mice are mated and the result is all gray offspring, what inheritance pattern would this be indicative of?
questions.explanation: An intermediate (blended) gray phenotype from black x white parents indicates incomplete dominance, where the heterozygote is a blend of the two homozygous phenotypes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q12). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Two nonstandard inheritance patterns are often confused: one produces a BLENDED intermediate in the heterozygote, the other shows BOTH parental phenotypes at once. In codominance, the heterozygote shows:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a smooth blend of the two phenotypes

hints.rationale: Codominance shows both parental phenotypes at once, not a single blended intermediate.

hints.prompt: The mouse cross gives offspring that are uniformly GRAY, a color intermediate between black and white. Is gray best described as 'both colors shown at once' or 'a blend'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: both colors shown separately at the same time

hints.rationale: Gray is a single intermediate blend of black and white, not the two colors displayed separately.

hints.prompt: A uniformly BLENDED color rules out patterns that would instead show a pure parental color or both colors separately. Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they predict a NON-blended result?
hints.rationale: Complete dominance predicts a pure parental color and codominance predicts both colors separately, so neither matches the observed blend.

questions.stem: The ABO blood groups in humans are expressed as the IA, IB, and i alleles. The IA allele encodes the A blood group antigen, IB encodes B, and i encodes O. Both A and B are dominant to O. If a heterozygous blood type A parent (IAi) and a heterozygous blood type B parent (IBi) mate, one quarter of their offspring will have AB blood type (IAIB) in which both antigens are expressed equally. Therefore, ABO blood groups are an example of:
questions.explanation: There are three alleles (multiple alleles), and IA and IB are codominant (both antigens expressed in AB individuals), so ABO illustrates multiple alleles and codominance.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q13). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: If a heterozygote expresses BOTH alleles' products fully and separately (not a blend), the relationship between those alleles is called:
hints.rationale: Full, separate expression of both alleles in the heterozygote is codominance.

hints.prompt: The ABO system uses three alleles (IA, IB, and i) in the population. A gene that has more than two allele versions in a population is described as having:
hints.rationale: Three allele versions (IA, IB, i) means the gene has multiple alleles.

hints.prompt: Which single answer choice is a trap because it describes ONLY a blended intermediate and ignores both the three alleles and the two fully-expressed antigens?
hints.rationale: 'Incomplete dominance only' is the trap: it captures neither the three alleles nor the full, separate expression of both antigens.

questions.stem: In a mating between two individuals that are heterozygous for a recessive lethal allele that is expressed in utero, what genotypic ratio (homozygous dominant:heterozygous:homozygous recessive) would you expect to observe in the offspring?
questions.explanation: An Aa x Aa cross gives a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio, but the homozygous recessive (aa) die in utero, so among observed offspring the ratio is 1:2:0.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q14). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified by Mendelian reasoning (aa lethal removes the homozygous recessive class).

hints.prompt: For a standard monohybrid cross between two heterozygotes (Aa x Aa), what genotypic ratio (homozygous dominant : heterozygous : homozygous recessive) is expected at conception?
hints.rationale: Aa x Aa yields a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio before any selection acts.

hints.prompt: A recessive LETHAL allele expressed in utero means the homozygous recessive (aa) individuals do what before they can be observed?
hints.rationale: A recessive lethal expressed in utero removes the homozygous recessive class before it can be observed.

hints.prompt: The homozygous-dominant and heterozygous classes are unaffected, so they stay in a 1:2 relationship. Which two answer choices can be eliminated because their FIRST two numbers are not 1 and 2?
hints.rationale: The dominant and heterozygous classes remain 1 and 2, so options starting 3:1 or 0:2 can be ruled out.

questions.stem: If the allele encoding polydactyly (six fingers) is dominant why do most people have five fingers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Genetic elements suppress the polydactyl gene.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sixth finger is removed at birth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The polydactyl allele is very rare in the human population.

questions.explanation: Dominance refers to how an allele is expressed, not how common it is. The polydactyly allele is rare, so most people simply do not carry it.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q15). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Does an allele being DOMINANT tell you anything about how COMMON it is in a population?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, dominant alleles are always common

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, dominance describes how an allele is expressed, not its frequency

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, dominant alleles are always rare

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, dominance sets the exact frequency

hints.rationale: Dominance is about how an allele is expressed, independent of how frequent it is.

hints.prompt: A dominant allele shows its phenotype whenever it is present, even in a single copy. Given that, why can't 'most people have five fingers' be explained by people hiding a dominant polydactyly allele?

choices.label: A
choices.text: because a dominant allele would be expressed if present, so people without the trait simply lack it

choices.label: B
choices.text: because dominant alleles are never expressed

choices.label: C
choices.text: because five fingers is itself the dominant trait

hints.rationale: A dominant allele is expressed whenever present, so people without the trait must not carry it.

hints.prompt: Which distractor is a trap because it would actually PREVENT affected people from ever existing, contradicting the fact that people with polydactyly clearly do exist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Genetic elements suppress the polydactyl gene.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sixth finger is removed at birth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The polydactyl allele is very rare in the human population.

hints.rationale: If polydactyly were embryonic lethal, affected individuals could not exist, yet they do, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: A farmer raises black and white chickens. To his surprise, when the first generation of eggs hatch all the chickens are black with white speckles throughout their feathers. What should the farmer expect when the eggs laid after interbreeding the speckled chickens hatch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All the offspring will be speckled.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 75% of the offspring will be speckled, and 25% will be black.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 50% of the offspring will be speckled, 25% will be black, and 25% will be white.

choices.label: D
choices.text: 50% of the offspring will be black and 50% of the offspring will be white.

questions.explanation: Speckled F1 indicates codominance (both black and white expressed). Interbreeding heterozygous speckled birds gives a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio: 25% black, 50% speckled, 25% white.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q16). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified by Mendelian reasoning (codominant 1:2:1).

hints.prompt: F1 chickens showing BOTH black and white (speckled) rather than a blended gray indicates which inheritance pattern?
hints.rationale: Displaying both colors at once (speckling) rather than a blend is codominance.

hints.prompt: The speckled F1 are heterozygotes. Crossing two heterozygotes (like Aa x Aa) produces what genotypic ratio?
hints.rationale: A heterozygote x heterozygote cross gives a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated, one because it wrongly claims the F2 are uniform (all one phenotype), and the other because it shows only the two pure colors with NO speckled birds?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'All the offspring will be speckled' and '50% black and 50% white'

choices.label: B
choices.text: '75% speckled, 25% black' and '50% speckled, 25% black, 25% white'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'All the offspring will be speckled' and '75% speckled, 25% black'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '50% black and 50% white' and '50% speckled, 25% black, 25% white'

hints.rationale: An all-speckled F2 (uniform) and a black/white-only F2 (no speckled) both contradict the expected three-class codominant result.

questions.stem: Assuming no gene linkage, in a dihybrid cross of AABB x aabb with AaBb F1 heterozygotes, what is the ratio of the F1 gametes (AB, aB, Ab, ab) that will give rise to the F2 offspring?
questions.explanation: By independent assortment, an AaBb individual produces the four gamete types AB, aB, Ab, and ab in equal proportions, a 1:1:1:1 ratio.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q17). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified by Mendelian reasoning.

hints.prompt: Under the law of independent assortment, when a dihybrid (AaBb) forms gametes, the alleles of the two genes combine:

choices.label: C
choices.text: always keeping A together with B

hints.rationale: Independent assortment means the two genes' alleles combine randomly and independently in gametes.

hints.prompt: Two distractor ratios describe F2 OFFSPRING patterns rather than GAMETE ratios. What is this question actually asking for?

choices.label: B
choices.text: the ratio of the F1 gametes

hints.rationale: The question asks for the ratio of F1 gamete types, not the F2 offspring ratio.

hints.prompt: One distractor, 1:2:2:1, resembles a genotype ratio rather than equal gamete frequencies. Which other distractor is a trap because it echoes the classic F2 OFFSPRING dihybrid pattern (as in 9:3:3:1)?
hints.rationale: 1:3:3:1 mimics the F2 offspring dihybrid pattern, so it is a trap for a gamete-ratio question.

questions.stem: The forked line and probability methods make use of what probability rule?
questions.explanation: These methods multiply the independent probabilities of each trait, applying the product rule of probability.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q18). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: To find the probability that TWO independent events BOTH occur, you combine their individual probabilities by:
hints.rationale: The probability that two independent events both occur is the product (multiply) of their probabilities.

hints.prompt: The forked-line and probability methods combine the chances of several independent traits occurring TOGETHER in one offspring. Combining 'AND' (all-together) probabilities requires which operation?
hints.rationale: Getting several traits together is an 'AND' situation, which multiplies the probabilities.

hints.prompt: The 'sum rule' (adding probabilities) is the trap here because it applies to a different situation. The sum rule is used when outcomes are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: required to happen together (an AND situation)

hints.rationale: The sum rule applies to mutually exclusive either/or events, not to both-together events, making it the trap.

questions.stem: How many different offspring genotypes are expected in a trihybrid cross between parents heterozygous for all three traits when the traits behave in a dominant and recessive pattern? How many phenotypes?
questions.explanation: For n heterozygous gene pairs there are 3^n genotypes and 2^n phenotypes. With n = 3: 3^3 = 27 genotypes and 2^3 = 8 phenotypes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q19). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified by calculation (3^3 genotypes, 2^3 phenotypes).

hints.prompt: For n gene pairs that are each heterozygous (with simple dominance), the number of possible offspring GENOTYPES is 3 to the n. The number of possible PHENOTYPES is:
hints.rationale: Each dominant/recessive trait has two phenotypes, so n traits give 2 to the n phenotypes.

hints.prompt: For any set of heterozygous gene pairs with simple dominance, will there be MORE possible genotypes or MORE possible phenotypes?
hints.rationale: Since 3 to the n exceeds 2 to the n, there are always more genotypes than phenotypes.

hints.prompt: You expect MORE genotypes than phenotypes. Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they list MORE phenotypes than genotypes?
hints.rationale: Options listing more phenotypes than genotypes invert the correct relationship and are ruled out.

questions.stem: Labrador retrievers' fur color is controlled by two alleles, E and B. Any dog with the ee__ genotype develops into a yellow lab, while B_E_ dogs become black labs and bbE_ dogs become chocolate labs. This is an example of _____.
questions.explanation: The ee genotype masks the B gene (yellow regardless of B/b), so one gene's alleles hide the expression of another gene — this is epistasis.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q20). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text (recessive epistasis).

hints.prompt: Codominance and incomplete dominance both describe interactions between the TWO ALLELES OF A SINGLE gene. Do they describe one gene masking a SECOND, different gene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, both describe one gene hiding another gene

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, they describe allele interactions within a single gene

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, they describe physical linkage on a chromosome

hints.rationale: Codominance and incomplete dominance are within-gene allele interactions, not one gene masking another gene.

hints.prompt: In Labradors, a dog with genotype ee is yellow NO MATTER what alleles it has at the separate B gene. This means the ee genotype is doing what to the B gene's effect?
hints.rationale: The ee genotype hides the B gene's effect, meaning one gene masks a second, different gene.

hints.prompt: Two answer choices describe interactions BETWEEN the two alleles of a SINGLE gene, not one gene hiding another. Which two can be eliminated on that basis?
hints.rationale: Codominance and incomplete dominance are single-gene allele interactions, so both are ruled out for a two-gene masking effect.

questions.stem: Which of the following situations does not follow the Law of Independent Assortment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A blond person and a brown-haired person produce three offspring over time, all of who have blond hair.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A white cow crossed with a brown bull produces roan cattle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mating a hog with a sow produces six female piglets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Men are more likely to experience hemophilia than women.

questions.explanation: Hemophilia is X-linked, so it is inherited with the sex chromosomes rather than assorting independently — a violation of independent assortment due to linkage.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 12 Mendel's Experiments and Heredity — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.12 review Q21). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text (sex-linked inheritance is an exception to independent assortment).

hints.prompt: The Law of Independent Assortment can fail when genes are inherited together with a particular chromosome. Traits inherited along with the SEX chromosomes are called:
hints.rationale: Traits inherited with the sex chromosomes are sex-linked, an exception to independent assortment.

hints.prompt: The scenario 'a hog and sow produce six female piglets' can look suspicious, but offspring sex results from random segregation of the sex chromosomes. Does an all-female litter VIOLATE independent assortment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, it proves the genes are linked

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, an offspring sex ratio is just chance and does not violate the law

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because sex is not heritable

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because piglets have no genes

hints.rationale: An all-female litter is ordinary chance in sex determination and does not violate independent assortment.

hints.prompt: Which two scenarios can be eliminated because they show ordinary inheritance (a dominant trait passing to offspring, and a codominant roan blend) that FOLLOWS independent assortment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the blond/brown-hair offspring result and the roan cattle result

choices.label: B
choices.text: the roan cattle result and the six female piglets

choices.label: C
choices.text: the blond/brown-hair result and the six female piglets

choices.label: D
choices.text: the six female piglets and the hemophilia pattern

hints.rationale: Simple dominance (the blond offspring) and codominance (the roan cattle) both follow normal assortment, so both are ruled out.

questions.stem: What is the difference between micro- and macroevolution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microevolution describes the evolution of small organisms, such as insects, while macroevolution describes the evolution of large organisms, like people and elephants.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Microevolution describes the evolution of microscopic entities, such as molecules and proteins, while macroevolution describes the evolution of whole organisms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Microevolution describes the evolution of organisms in populations, while macroevolution describes the evolution of species over long periods of time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Microevolution describes the evolution of organisms over their lifetimes, while macroevolution describes the evolution of organisms over multiple generations.

questions.explanation: Microevolution is change in allele frequencies within populations; macroevolution refers to larger-scale patterns such as speciation over long timescales.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q4). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Evolution is defined as a change in what, over generations, which is the basis for distinguishing micro- from macroevolution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: an individual organism's body during its own lifetime

choices.label: D
choices.text: the number of molecules in a cell

hints.rationale: Evolution is change in allele frequencies across generations, not change within one organism's lifetime.

hints.prompt: Given that evolution happens to POPULATIONS across generations (never within one organism's lifetime), which distractor can be dismissed for describing change 'over their lifetimes'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the option contrasting small vs. large organisms

choices.label: B
choices.text: the option contrasting molecules vs. whole organisms

choices.label: C
choices.text: the option saying microevolution occurs over an organism's lifetime

choices.label: D
choices.text: none of the options can be dismissed

hints.rationale: Evolution never occurs within a single organism's lifetime, so that option is wrong.

hints.prompt: Micro- and macroevolution are distinguished by SCALE (populations vs. species over time), not by organism SIZE or by molecules-vs-organisms. Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they wrongly define the terms that way?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the 'small vs. large organisms' option and the 'molecules vs. whole organisms' option

choices.label: B
choices.text: the 'populations vs. species' option and the 'lifetimes vs. generations' option

choices.label: C
choices.text: the 'small vs. large organisms' option and the 'populations vs. species' option

choices.label: D
choices.text: the 'molecules vs. whole organisms' option and the 'lifetimes vs. generations' option

hints.rationale: Defining the terms by organism size or by molecules-versus-organisms misrepresents both, so those two are ruled out.

questions.stem: Population genetics is the study of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: how selective forces change the allele frequencies in a population over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: the genetic basis of population-wide traits

choices.label: C
choices.text: whether traits have a genetic basis

choices.label: D
choices.text: the degree of inbreeding in a population

questions.explanation: Population genetics studies how evolutionary forces, including selection, change allele frequencies in populations over time.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q5). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Population genetics focuses on how the frequencies of what change in a population across time?
hints.rationale: Population genetics tracks how allele frequencies change over time.

hints.prompt: Evolutionary forces such as natural selection act to CHANGE allele frequencies. Population genetics is especially concerned with which aspect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the unchanging, static state of a population

choices.label: B
choices.text: the dynamic change of frequencies over time

choices.label: C
choices.text: a purely descriptive snapshot of one individual

hints.rationale: Population genetics emphasizes how allele frequencies change over time, driven by forces like selection.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they concern WHETHER traits are genetic or how inbred a population is, rather than CHANGES in allele frequency over time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'how selective forces change allele frequencies' and 'the degree of inbreeding'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'whether traits have a genetic basis' and 'the degree of inbreeding in a population'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'how selective forces change allele frequencies' and 'the genetic basis of population-wide traits'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the genetic basis of population-wide traits' and 'how selective forces change allele frequencies'

hints.rationale: Asking whether traits are genetic, or measuring inbreeding, is not about allele-frequency change over time.

questions.stem: One of the original Amish colonies rose from a ship of colonists that came from Europe. The ship's captain, who had polydactyly, a rare dominant trait, was one of the original colonists. Today, we see a much higher frequency of polydactyly in the Amish population. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: The founder effect is a form of genetic drift that occurs when a small group establishes a new population, so both 'genetic drift' and 'founder effect' apply.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q7). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text (founder effect is a type of genetic drift).

hints.prompt: When allele frequencies change purely by CHANCE — for example because a new population is started by a small, random sample of individuals — rather than because certain alleles improve survival, the change is driven by:
hints.rationale: Allele-frequency change from random sampling in a small group is due to chance, not a survival advantage.

hints.prompt: A single colonist carrying a rare allele leads to its high frequency in the new population by chance, not by any survival advantage. Which force is therefore NOT responsible here?
hints.rationale: The change is due to chance in a small founding group, not a survival advantage, so natural selection is not the cause.

hints.prompt: Which answer choice is the trap that should be rejected because the allele became common by CHANCE in a small founding group, not because it improved survival or reproduction?
hints.rationale: Natural selection is the trap; the allele spread by chance in a small founding group, not by a fitness advantage.

questions.stem: When male lions reach sexual maturity, they leave their group in search of a new pride. This can alter the allele frequencies of the population through which of the following mechanisms?
questions.explanation: Migration of individuals between populations moves alleles from one to another, which is gene flow.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q8). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: When organisms MIGRATE from one population and breed in another, what do they carry with them that can change the receiving population's allele frequencies?
hints.rationale: Migrating individuals carry their alleles into the new population, altering its allele frequencies.

hints.prompt: A mature male lion LEAVING his group to join a different pride is an example of an individual doing what between populations?
hints.rationale: Leaving one group to join another is migration between populations.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they do NOT require any movement of individuals between populations?
hints.rationale: Natural selection and genetic drift can occur within a single population without any migration, so both are ruled out.

questions.stem: Which of the following evolutionary forces can introduce new genetic variation into a population?
questions.explanation: Mutation creates new alleles, and gene flow brings in alleles from other populations; both add new genetic variation. Selection and drift act on existing variation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q9). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Evolutionary processes differ: some can introduce genetic variation that was never present before, while others only re-sort or remove variation that already exists. Which SINGLE process can introduce a brand-new allele that did not previously exist in any population?
hints.rationale: Mutation generates brand-new alleles, whereas selection, drift, and nonrandom mating only act on variation that already exists.

hints.prompt: Natural selection and genetic drift change the frequencies of alleles that ALREADY exist. Can they, by themselves, introduce a brand-new allele into the population?

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, they only act on existing variation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, both of them create new alleles

hints.rationale: Selection and drift only act on existing alleles; they do not create new ones.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because NEITHER of their listed processes introduces new variation (both merely act on existing alleles)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'natural selection and genetic drift' and 'natural selection and nonrandom mating'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'mutation and gene flow' and 'mutation and genetic drift'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'natural selection and genetic drift' and 'mutation and gene flow'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'mutation and genetic drift' and 'natural selection and nonrandom mating'

hints.rationale: Selection, drift, and nonrandom mating all act only on existing alleles, so the two options built entirely from them add nothing new.

questions.stem: What is assortative mating?

choices.label: A
choices.text: when individuals mate with those who are similar to themselves

choices.label: B
choices.text: when individuals mate with those who are dissimilar to themselves

choices.label: C
choices.text: when individuals mate with those who are the most fit in the population

choices.label: D
choices.text: when individuals mate with those who are least fit in the population

questions.explanation: Assortative mating is nonrandom mating in which individuals preferentially mate with phenotypically similar partners.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q10). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Assortative mating is a form of NONRANDOM mating in which mate choice is based on:

choices.label: B
choices.text: phenotypic similarity or dissimilarity between mates

hints.rationale: Assortative mating is nonrandom mating based on how similar or dissimilar potential mates' phenotypes are.

hints.prompt: Two answer choices define mate choice by FITNESS ('most fit' or 'least fit') rather than by resemblance. Does assortative mating refer to choosing mates by their fitness level?

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, it refers to resemblance (similarity or dissimilarity), not a fitness ranking

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, fitness is the only factor

hints.rationale: Assortative mating is about phenotypic resemblance, not about selecting mates by fitness.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they describe choosing mates based on FITNESS (most or least fit) rather than on resemblance?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the most fit in the population' and 'the least fit in the population'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'similar to themselves' and 'the most fit in the population'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'dissimilar to themselves' and 'the least fit in the population'

hints.rationale: The 'most fit' and 'least fit' options describe fitness-based choice, not the resemblance that defines assortative mating.

questions.stem: When closely related individuals mate with each other, or inbreed, the offspring are often not as fit as the offspring of two unrelated individuals. Why?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The DNA of close relatives reacts negatively in the offspring.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inbreeding can bring together rare, deleterious mutations that lead to harmful phenotypes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inbreeding causes normally silent alleles to be expressed.

questions.explanation: Inbreeding increases homozygosity, making it more likely that rare recessive deleterious alleles pair up and produce harmful phenotypes (inbreeding depression).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q11). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Inbreeding increases the chance that offspring inherit TWO copies of the same allele. In other words, inbreeding increases:
hints.rationale: Mating between close relatives increases homozygosity in the offspring.

hints.prompt: Many harmful alleles are RECESSIVE, so they cause harm only when present in TWO copies. Increased homozygosity from inbreeding therefore makes it more likely that:

choices.label: B
choices.text: two copies of a harmful recessive allele come together

choices.label: D
choices.text: chromosomes fail to form at all

hints.rationale: Higher homozygosity raises the chance that two harmful recessive alleles pair up and are expressed.

hints.prompt: One distractor claims inbreeding 'causes normally silent alleles to be expressed.' Why is that phrasing misleading?

choices.label: A
choices.text: because harmful recessive alleles are not 'silent'; they are simply masked until two are paired in a homozygote

choices.label: B
choices.text: because alleles can never be expressed

choices.label: D
choices.text: because all alleles are always expressed regardless

hints.rationale: Recessive deleterious alleles are not truly silent; they are masked by a dominant allele until inbreeding pairs two together.

questions.stem: What is a cline?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the slope of a mountain where a population lives

choices.label: B
choices.text: the degree to which a mutation helps an individual survive

choices.label: C
choices.text: the number of individuals in the population

choices.label: D
choices.text: gradual geographic variation across an ecological gradient

questions.explanation: A cline is a gradual change in a trait or allele frequency across a geographic or ecological gradient.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q12). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: The concept of a 'cline' involves a GRADIENT. In general, does a gradient describe something that stays uniform and constant, or something that changes progressively along an axis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: something that stays uniform and constant

choices.label: B
choices.text: something that changes progressively along an axis

choices.label: C
choices.text: something that is counted as a single total

choices.label: D
choices.text: something randomly scattered with no pattern

hints.rationale: A gradient refers to a progressive change along an axis, not a constant value, a total count, or a random scatter.

hints.prompt: The distractor 'the slope of a mountain where a population lives' plays on the everyday meaning of the word. Is a biological cline a literal physical landform?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, it is literally a mountain slope

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, a cline is not a physical landform

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only when populations live on mountains

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a cline is actually a headcount of organisms

hints.rationale: A cline is a biological pattern, not a literal physical landform like a mountain slope.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they describe a COUNT of individuals or a measure of SURVIVAL benefit, neither of which is a gradual geographic pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'the slope of a mountain' and 'gradual geographic variation across a gradient'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the degree to which a mutation helps survival' and 'the number of individuals in the population'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'the slope of a mountain' and 'the number of individuals in the population'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the degree to which a mutation helps survival' and 'gradual geographic variation across a gradient'

hints.rationale: A survival-benefit measure and a population count are not gradual geographic patterns, so both are ruled out.

questions.stem: Which type of selection results in greater genetic variability in a population?
questions.explanation: Diversifying (disruptive) selection favors both extremes over intermediate phenotypes, increasing genetic variability in the population.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q13). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: A type of selection that favors BOTH extreme phenotypes over the intermediate ones will tend to do what to the population's genetic variability?
hints.rationale: Favoring both extremes preserves and can increase genetic variability.

hints.prompt: Stabilizing selection favors the AVERAGE/intermediate phenotype and removes the extremes. What effect does stabilizing selection have on variability?

choices.label: C
choices.text: it has no effect on variability

hints.rationale: Stabilizing selection favors the mean and reduces variability by removing extremes.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they NARROW variation, one by favoring the average and the other by shifting the population toward a SINGLE extreme?
hints.rationale: Stabilizing selection favors the mean and directional selection shifts toward one extreme; both reduce variability.

questions.stem: When males and females of a population look or act differently, it is referred to as ________.
questions.explanation: Sexual dimorphism is the difference in appearance or behavior between males and females of the same species.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q14). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Some biology terms describe a PATTERN or STATE (a difference that simply exists), while others describe a PROCESS or FORCE (something that causes change). 'Sexual selection' is best categorized as:

choices.label: B
choices.text: a process/force that drives evolutionary change

hints.rationale: Sexual selection is an evolutionary process/force, not merely a description of how the sexes look.

hints.prompt: The question asks for the term describing the STATE in which males and females simply LOOK or ACT differently, an observed difference rather than a driving force. Should the answer be a 'force/process' term or a 'descriptive state' term?
hints.rationale: 'Males and females look or act differently' describes an observed state, so a descriptive term fits, not a process term.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they have NOTHING to do with differences between males and females (one is about phenotypes at the population's extremes, the other about geographic gradients)?
hints.rationale: Diversifying selection concerns extreme phenotypes and a cline is a geographic gradient; neither involves male/female differences.

questions.stem: The good genes hypothesis is a theory that explains what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: why more fit individuals are more likely to have more offspring

choices.label: B
choices.text: why alleles that confer beneficial traits or behaviors are selected for by natural selection

choices.label: C
choices.text: why some deleterious mutations are maintained in the population

choices.label: D
choices.text: why individuals of one sex develop impressive ornamental traits

questions.explanation: The good genes hypothesis proposes that elaborate ornaments are honest signals of genetic quality, explaining why one sex evolves impressive ornamental traits that the other sex prefers.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 19 The Evolution of Populations — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.19 review Q15). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: The 'good genes hypothesis' is part of the theory of SEXUAL SELECTION. It proposes that elaborate ornaments (like a peacock's tail) serve as honest signals of an individual's:
hints.rationale: Under the good genes hypothesis, ornaments honestly advertise underlying genetic quality.

hints.prompt: The good genes hypothesis specifically addresses MATE CHOICE and ornaments, not survival in general. Which broad topic is it NOT primarily trying to explain?

choices.label: B
choices.text: the routine survival advantage of any beneficial trait in general

hints.rationale: It focuses on ornaments and mate choice, not the general survival advantage of any beneficial trait.

hints.prompt: Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they describe GENERAL natural selection (fitness or beneficial alleles) rather than mate choice and ornaments?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'why more fit individuals have more offspring' and 'why beneficial alleles are selected for'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'why some deleterious mutations are maintained' and 'why one sex develops ornamental traits'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'why more fit individuals have more offspring' and 'why one sex develops ornamental traits'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'why beneficial alleles are selected for' and 'why some deleterious mutations are maintained'

hints.rationale: General fitness and selection for beneficial alleles are ordinary natural selection, not the ornament-focused good genes hypothesis.
